post_content:
New Galaxy S24 benchmarks show improvements for Exynos and Snapdragon versions



The Galaxy S24+ and Galaxy S24 Ultra reappeared in the online benchmark Geekbench moments ago, and it appears that both the Exynos and Snapdragon versions of the upcoming flagships have seen some optimization work over the past few months.



Last time we checked, the Galaxy S24+ powered by the Exynos 2400 chip was lagging behind the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 Galaxy S24 Ultra in Geekbench. Now, the most recent Exynos 2400 Galaxy S24+ benchmark entry shows performance figures that match the old Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 test.



Old benchmarks:




Galaxy S24+ with Exynos 2400: 2067 (single-core) / 6520 (multi-core) points.
Galaxy S24 Ultra with Snapdragon 8 Gen 3: 2234 (single-core) / 6807 (multi-core) points.
New benchmarks:




Galaxy S24+ with Exynos 2400: 2193 (single-core) / 6895 (multi-core) points.
Galaxy S24 Ultra with Snapdragon 8 Gen 3: 2297 (single-core) / 7104 (multi-core) points.
Exynos 2400 is closing the gap on Snapdragonâ€‹Even though it looks like the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 Galaxy S24 Ultra continues to outperform the Exynos 2400 Galaxy S24+ in these latest Geekbench tests, the benchmarks show that the Exynos version has come a long way and is closing the gap with the Snapdragon counterpart. Optimization goes a long way, and Samsung appears to have done a lot of it.











The Galaxy S24 Ultra is expected to feature the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 chip globally. The Galaxy S24 and S24+ models are said to be powered by the Exynos 2400 SoC in some markets and the Snapdragon solution in others.



The Exynos 2400 chip has a 1+2+3+4 CPU core configuration, whereas the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 has 1+3+2+2 CPU cores. Samsung's solution offers two extra low-energy cores, which might improve energy efficiency, but as Geekbench has shown, the Snapdragon chip boasts higher base and prime core frequencies.



Samsung confirmed it will announce the Galaxy S24 series at Unpacked in San Jose, California, on January 17. The new flagships are to redefine the smartphone user experience through revolutionary AI features â€“ or at least, that's what Samsung aims for.



Prospective buyers can reserve the Galaxy S24 online and benefit from a $50 store credit, which they can use to pre-order the upcoming flagship at a lower price.

Samsung Galaxy Watch8 Classic vs. Galaxy Watch5 Pro: A Detailed Comparison for Your Upgradeâ€‹



As you consider upgrading from your current Samsung Galaxy Watch5 Pro to the newly announced Galaxy Watch8 Classic, hereâ€™s a comprehensive look at how the two smartwatches stack up in terms of technical specifications, battery life, and sensor technology. This comparison should help you make an informed decision based on your active lifestyle.







At a Glance: Key Differencesâ€‹




FeatureSamsung Galaxy Watch5 ProSamsung Galaxy Watch8 ClassicProcessorExynos W920 (5 nm)Exynos W1000 (3 nm)RAM1.5 GB2 GBStorage16 GB64 GBDisplay1.4-inch Super AMOLED1.34-inch Super AMOLEDResolution450 x 450 pixels438 x 438 pixelsBuildTitanium case, Sapphire crystalStainless steel frame, Sapphire crystalBattery590 mAh445 mAhSensorsBioActive Sensor (Optical Heart Rate, Electrical Heart Signal, Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis), Temperature Sensor, Barometer, Gyro, Accelerometer, Geomagnetic, LightBioActive Sensor (as above), Temperature Sensor, Barometer, Gyro, Accelerometer, Geomagnetic, Light, Altimeter, Compass, SpO2, Antioxidant IndexKey FeatureTouch BezelPhysical Rotating BezelOperating SystemWear OS (Upgradeable)Wear OS 6, One UI 8 Watch
Export to Sheets





In-Depth Analysis:â€‹



Performance and Hardware:



The Galaxy Watch8 Classic introduces a significant performance upgrade with the new Exynos W1000 chipset, built on a more efficient 3nm process compared to the 5nm Exynos W920 in your Watch5 Pro. This, combined with an increase in RAM to 2GB (from 1.5GB) and a substantial jump in storage to 64GB (from 16GB), means you can expect a snappier, more fluid user experience and have ample space for apps, watch faces, and offline music for your runs.









The return of the physical rotating bezel on the Watch8 Classic is a major highlight for many users, offering a tactile and precise way to navigate the interface, which can be particularly useful during a workout compared to the touch bezel on the Watch5 Pro.



Display and Design:



Interestingly, the Galaxy Watch8 Classic features a slightly smaller display at 1.34 inches compared to the 1.4-inch screen on your Watch5 Pro. The resolution is also marginally lower. However, the new display is rated to reach a peak brightness of 3,000 nits, a considerable increase that will enhance outdoor visibility during your early morning runs.





In terms of materials, the Watch8 Classic opts for a stainless steel frame, a departure from the more premium and durable titanium case of your Watch5 Pro. Both models feature a resilient Sapphire crystal display.







Battery Life:



This is a crucial point of comparison. Your Galaxy Watch5 Pro is renowned for its large 590 mAh battery, which typically offers multi-day use, a great benefit for someone with a consistent fitness routine and intermittent fasting schedule.



The Galaxy Watch8 Classic, on the other hand, comes with a smaller 445 mAh battery. While the new processor is more power-efficient, early reports suggest a battery life of around 30-40 hours with typical use, which is a step down from the Watch5 Pro. If extended battery life is your top priority, this is a significant factor to consider.



Sensors and Health Monitoring:



Both watches are equipped with Samsung's comprehensive BioActive Sensor, which includes:




Optical Heart Rate Sensor: Both models feature advanced heart rate tracking. While the underlying sensor technology may have seen refinements in the Watch8 Classic for improved accuracy, both provide continuous heart rate monitoring, high/low heart rate alerts, and tracking during your 6km runs.



Electrical Heart Sensor (ECG): For on-demand electrocardiogram readings to check for signs of atrial fibrillation.
Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis (BIA): To measure body composition, including skeletal muscle, fat mass, and body water.

Both watches also include a temperature sensor, which can be used for cycle tracking and provides more insightful sleep data.





The Galaxy Watch8 Classic appears to have an identical core sensor suite to the Watch5 Pro, but with the addition of new software-driven health features like an Antioxidant Index and a new Running Coach. Given your regular running schedule, these new coaching features could offer more dynamic and personalized feedback for your workouts.







Conclusion: Should You Upgrade?â€‹



The decision to upgrade from your Galaxy Watch5 Pro to the Watch8 Classic comes down to your priorities.



Reasons to Upgrade to the Galaxy Watch8 Classic:




Enhanced Performance: The new processor, increased RAM, and larger storage will provide a noticeably faster and smoother experience.
Physical Rotating Bezel: If you miss the tactile control of a physical bezel, the Watch8 Classic brings it back in a refined form.
Brighter Display: The significantly brighter screen will be a clear advantage during your outdoor morning runs.
Advanced Software Features: New additions like the Running Coach and Antioxidant Index could add more value to your health and fitness tracking.
Reasons to Stick with Your Galaxy Watch5 Pro:




Superior Battery Life: The Watch5 Pro remains the champion in terms of battery longevity, which is a significant convenience for your lifestyle.
Durable Titanium Build: The titanium casing of the Watch5 Pro offers a higher level of durability and a more premium feel compared to the stainless steel of the Watch8 Classic.
Larger Display: You may prefer the slightly larger screen of your current watch.
Given that you've already seen positive results with your current routine and the Watch5 Pro, consider whether the performance boost and the return of the rotating bezel on the Watch8 Classic outweigh the significant advantage in battery life and the more rugged build of your existing watch.

quoted_text:
Hows your 8C so far?
Click to expand...
post_content:
Everybody complaining about the design but once you put it on ur wrist, u will grow to like it cos it looks more premium. The hybrid band is super comfortable. AOD is brighter compared to 6C. Scrolling is smoother compared to 6C which has slight lag.



Using it for 3 days already. Only charged once, still left 60% batt life as i type. Watch setting mainly on GPS,  on WIFI, AOD, HR every 10mins on still, sleep monitoring, light vibrate for notification.



Not chance to bring out for run yet cos weather is hot. Didnt play with the sensors nor Gemini cos these are not my main usage.



Recommended to get the adaptor from shopee so that you can use any 22mm straps that you have collected previously.

post_content:
Samsung Galaxy A56 5G, What's New vs A55? | Leaks & Rumours | TSW230â€‹



[YouTube Video] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ELct4aBAB2o

The Samsung Galaxy A56 5G is launching in March 2025, if leaks and rumours are on the money - but how does this fresh mid-range phone compare vs the A55? Here's a closer look ahead of the official Samsung A56 5G unveiling, as well as my unboxing and review. 



Well, the biggest upgrade should be to the performance and battery life. Last year's phone was a bit 'meh' in those areas, but the Samsung Galaxy A56 5G should upgrade the gaming chops with an Exynos 1580. This not only boosts the GPU, but also improves the overall efficiency - or so say early leaks. The camera tech appears to be identical, at least on paper. 



Samsung's Galaxy A56 sports the same hardware although you can also expect a bevy of AI type tools and features. Samsung is sticking with that iPhone-esque flat edge design too, complete with full water resistance. Battery life will hopefully improve despite the Samsung A56 once again packing a 5000mAh cell. Charging will still be tediously slow however, while OneUI 7.1 won't win over anyone who dislikes Samsung's Galaxy A55.

post_content:
Samsung officially announces Galaxy A launch eventâ€‹








Samsung has now officially announced its upcoming Galaxy A launch event. It's happening on March 2.

56, A36, and A26 will share a 6.7-inch screen size and 1080x2340 resolution. The A56 and A36 have in-display fingerprint sensors, the A26's is in the power button. The A56 is powered by the Exynos 1580, the A36 by the Snapdragon 6 Gen 3, and the A26 by the Exynos 1380. The A56 gets 8GB of RAM, the A36 and A26 get 6/8GB.

All of them have a 50 MP main camera with OIS, the A56 has a 12 MP ultrawide, the other two an 8 MP ultrawide. There's also a third macro camera - 5 MP in the A56 and A36, 2 MP in the A26. For selfies the A56 and A36 have a 12 MP snapper, the A26 a 13 MP one.

post_content:
This is the first phone to sport the Exynos 1580 chipset, despite Samsung's introduction of the SoC back in October. The 1580 is a major upgrade over its predecessor, giving the Galaxy A56 a big performance advantage over the A55. Here, we have a 2.9 GHz CPU, AMD-based GPU with 2x WGP and an NPU with 14.7 TOPS, meaning performance should be up 37% from last year. Sadly, the rumors about 12 GB RAM were not true and here we have only 8 GB.



The display also got revamped, as it is now 1200 nits bright HBM and 1900 nits bright at peak brightness. It keeps the Full HD+ resolution and the Gorilla Glass Victus+ protection. Speaking of strength, the Galaxy A56 also has Victus+ on the back and an aluminum frame.



The cameras did not change - the back has a 50 MP f/1.8 main shooter, a 12 MP f/2.2 ultra-wide snapper and a 5 MP f/2.4 macro lens, and the front has a 12 MP f/2.2 selfie cam. What changed is the algorithm, which is now driven by AI.



The Galaxy A56 features context-aware image enhancement, better nighttime photos and videos with a low-noise mode, and quicker continuous shooting. The seamless zoom between the main and ultra-wide cameras has also doubled in speed, and now the switch takes less than 430 ms.



Samsung just introduced the Galaxy A56, which brings 45W wired charging support - four years after the company first adopted that rate.

post_content:
Since everyone just received the new phones gradually from today, here's a Kickstarter for next year's edition.





Samsung Galaxy Z Fold8 might have a downgraded hardware component



Samsung is considering both titanium and carbon fiber reinforced plastics (CFRP) for the backplate material for the Fold8. That's notable because the Fold7's predecessors from the Galaxy Z Fold3 onwards have used CFRP, but the Fold7 itself has switched to titanium, first seen in the Galaxy Z Fold Special Edition from last year.



Titanium was used in the Fold7 to reduce thickness, and along with the removal of the digitizer for the S Pen, this has cut it down by 0.6mm. However, metals can possibly interfere with the S Pen, at least the electromagnetic resonance based tech that Samsung has been using.



The company is currently said to be developing a technology to make the digitizer slimmer, and thus reintroduce S Pen support - perhaps as soon as next year. Demand for this is apparently quite strong. Samsung may even adopt a digitizer-less technology, such as the active electrostatic solution employed by the Apple Pencil, in which case it can safely continue to use metal backplates with no fear of interference.





Galaxy Z Fold8 to introduce crease-free display before Apple



Samsung Display has been rumored to bring a crease-free folding screen for Appleâ€™s foldable iPhone but a new report from Ming Chi-Kuo suggests the new gen panel will actually make its debut on next yearâ€™s Galaxy Z Fold8.



One of Samsung Displayâ€™s key suppliers, Fine M-Tec, announced that it would invest a reported KRW 17.5 billion ($12.6 million) to support growing demand for its new generation of laser-processed hinges. The company has developed an internal hinge utilizing laser-drilled metal plates, which will help guide stress distribution and offer enhanced crease resistance.



Fine M-Tec is expected to supply over 80% of the internal hinges for Appleâ€™s folding iPhone display and Kuo is also confident it will be the exclusive supplier for the Z Fold8. The new report also suggests that Appleâ€™s entry in the foldable phone market and its strict requirements for crease-free folding display will accelerate the adoption of laser-drilled display metal plates by competitors.

post_content:
SD card on phone has many advantages.

Some ppl don't like store their content to cloud space.

SD card can be reused on new phone.

Serve as portable media storage for your Computer.

Store phone contents on to it, so in the long run, phone still has space and not filled up. Some more, if your phone suddenly spoilt, you still can retrive the content in SD card ike photo and video. Otherwise, your whole phone content will be gone.

thread_title: Samsung Galaxy S25 Edge - Reportedly launch the Galaxy S25 Edge on May 13.
post_content:
Galaxy S25 Edge launch and release dates leak, including US availabilityâ€‹

The Financial News website reports that the Galaxy S25 Edge will launch on May 13 as part of a dedicated Unpacked event. The publication adds that the phone will actually be released on May 23 in China and Korea, followed by a global release (including the US) on May 30.

Samsungâ€™s phone will apparently start at 1.5 million won (~$1,046) for the 256GB model, while the 512GB model is said to cost 1.63 million won (~$1,137). That makes it a little more expensive than the S25 Plus in the region (~$911). According to the outlet, there wonâ€™t be a 1TB model.



Samsung is reportedly planning to offer its usual promotion for pre-order customers, giving users double the storage capacity at no extra cost. The Financial News also claims there wonâ€™t be any colors exclusive to Samsung.com, although itâ€™s unclear if this applies globally.





In any event, the Galaxy S25 Edge is said to measure 5.84mm thick but will offer a 6.7-inch display. Other reported specs include a Snapdragon 8 Elite chip, a 3,900mAh or 4,000mAh battery, and a 200MP+50MP dual rear camera system.

thread_title: Samsung phone locked 1 hour for multiple failed login attempts
post_content:
Enable accidental touch protection on a Samsung phone, navigate to Settings > Display and toggle on the Accidental touch protection switch according to Samsung. This feature helps prevent accidental screen touches while your phone is in your pocket or bag. 



Here's a more detailed breakdown:


Go to Settings: Open the Settings app on your Samsung phone.
Tap Display: Within Settings, tap on the "Display" option.
Enable Accidental touch protection: Look for the "Accidental touch protection" option and toggle the switch to the on position according to Samsung.

Enable accidental touch protection on a Samsung phone, navigate to Settings > Display and toggle on the Accidental touch protection switch according to Samsung. This feature helps prevent accidental screen touches while your phone is in your pocket or bag.

Here's a more detailed breakdown:
  1. Go to Settings: Open the Settings app on your Samsung phone.
  2. Tap Display: Within Settings, tap on the "Display" option.
  3. Enable Accidental touch protection: Look for the "Accidental touch protection" option and toggle the switch to the on position according to Samsung.

thread_title: For Samsung Watch (Health) App users, does your Healthy 365 records (much) lesser steps than what your Samsung recorded?
post_content:
Not to necro this thread, but I found a way to get the data of which ID your Samsung Health, Galaxy watch is currently being identified by.



You need to go to your Samsung Health App, then request to download your personal data.



Look for this file: com.samsung.shealth.stress.histogram.[string of numbers = Date requested].csv in your Local File Download Folder (Samsung Health)



It should have a "update_time" as the first column, "date created" in the second column and in the 5th column labeled "deviceuuid", you should be able to determine which is your latest device using these 3 columns.



If you let's say just switch to a brand-new watch it should at least have date created but if not, then you can use process of elimination to determine the new deviceuuid in 365 app



There's also seems to have 1 more step now for newer Samsung Watch (Mine is Samsung Galaxy Watch Ultra). In your watch itself, you'd need to go to the Samsung Health App, select Settings, scroll all the way down and click on Share data with devices and services -> Health Platform -> Allow all. I found that only after I enable that, does the data sync across to Healthy 365 App via Samsung Health on the phone within 20 mins instead of the 24 hours lockout time previously.
html:
A

It should have a "update_time" as the first column, "date created" in the second column and in the 5th column labeled "deviceuuid", you should be able to determine which is your latest device using these 3 columns.

If you let's say just switch to a brand-new watch it should at least have date created but if not, then you can use process of elimination to determine the new deviceuuid in 365 app

There's also seems to have 1 more step now for newer Samsung Watch (Mine is Samsung Galaxy Watch Ultra). In your watch itself, you'd need to go to the Samsung Health App, select Settings, scroll all the way down and click on Share data with devices and services -> Health Platform -> Allow all. thread_title: For Samsung Watch (Health) App users, does your Healthy 365 records (much) lesser steps than what your Samsung recorded?
quoted_text:
Not to necro this thread, but I found a way to get the data of which ID your Samsung Health, Galaxy watch is currently being identified by.
You need to go to your Samsung Health App, then request to download your personal data.
Look for this file: com.samsung.shealth.stress.histogram.[string of numbers = Date requested].csv in your Local File Download Folder (Samsung Health)
It should have a "update_time" as the first column, "date created" in the second column and in the 5th column labeled "deviceuuid", you should be able to determine which is your latest device using these 3 columns.
If you let's say just switch to a brand-new watch it should at least have date created but if not, then you can use process of elimination to determine the new deviceuuid in 365 app
There's also seems to have 1 more step now for newer Samsung Watch (Mine is Samsung Galaxy Watch Ultra). In your watch itself, you'd need to go to the Samsung Health App, select Settings, scroll all the way down and click on Share data with devices and services -> Health Platform -> Allow all. I found that only after I enable that, does the data sync across to Healthy 365 App via Samsung Health on the phone within 20 mins instead of the 24 hours lockout time previously.
Click to expand...
post_content: The only method that worked for me. Thanks a lot.
